channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Former Chinese Billionaire and Bannon Associate Is Sentenced for Fraud
item.description: The businessman courted U.S. conservatives as an anti-Communist crusader while using his supporters’ money to buy lavish homes and a Bugatti supercar.
item.dc:creator: Michael Forsythe
item.media:credit: James Estrin/The New York Times
item.media:description: Guo Wengui at his Manhattan apartment in 2017.

item.title: Two Dan Sullivans to Appear on Alaska Senate Ballot, State High Court Rules
item.description: Republicans had sought the removal of a little-known candidate with the same name as the incumbent senator, arguing that he was not a “good faith” candidate.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Katie Holmlund/Associated Press; Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Republicans unsuccessfully sought to block a little known candidate, Dan J. Sullivan, left, from the same ballot as their party’s incumbent, Senator Dan S. Sullivan, right.

item.title: Kean Set to Speak at the Capitol After Mysterious Absence
item.description: After months of silence, Representative Tom Kean Jr. was expected to deliver a speech explaining his 116-day absence from Congress.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Bryan Anselm for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Tom Kean Jr. in Bridgewater, N.J., in 2024.

item.title: Senator Gallego’s Use of Campaign Funds for Family Trips Prompts Scrutiny
item.description: The Arizona senator used campaign money to fly his family to Nantucket and the Caribbean. The Justice Department has said it is investigating his campaign finance activity.
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner and Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Ruben Gallego of Arizona repeatedly took his family to vacation destinations and billed tens of thousands of dollars in flights to his campaign accounts just in 2025.

item.title: Trump on Legislation to Address the Nation’s Housing Crisis: ‘It’s a Yawn’
item.description: Time and again, President Trump has brushed off Americans’ concerns about the economy and their financial situations.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said on Monday that he had yet to receive a bill intended to lower housing costs and that he was not sure he would sign it once he did.

item.title: What to Watch in Colorado Primary Elections: Will Left-Wing Energy Keep Rising?
item.description: Primaries on Tuesday will be the latest test of Democrats’ anti-establishment mood.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Chet Strange for The New York Times; David Zalubowski/Associated Press

item.title: Emily Barker Loved Fighting Fires. She Died in One She Couldn’t Escape.
item.description: Emily Barker was one of two women who died fighting a large forest fire in Colorado this week.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy
item.media:credit: via Lisa Karczewski
item.media:description: Emily Barker loved the outdoors and crossed the country doing prescribed burns and fighting fires each summer.

item.title: Clash Unfolds as Trump Administration Pushes Intelligence Agencies to Share Foreign Espionage Targets
item.description: The clash reflects increasing skepticism of the Office of the Director of National Intelligence, which has been willing to pursue President Trump’s priorities.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett, William K. Rashbaum and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:credit: Erin Schaff/The New York Times
item.media:description: The headquarters of the Federal Bureau of Investigation in Washington. The Office of the Director of National Intelligence is asking the F.B.I. and the C.I.A. to provide a complete list of counterintelligence agents.

item.title: Newborn’s Body Is Found in a Portable Bathroom at Electric Forest Music Festival
item.description: Investigators were asking for information from people who attended the Electric Forest music festival, which draws tens of thousands of people to Western Michigan.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Joel Bissell/Muskegon Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The campground at Electric Forest, a four-day music festival, in 2022.

item.title: Ethics Panel Tosses Misconduct Claims Against Gallego
item.description: The Ethics Committee told Senator Ruben Gallego it had dismissed allegations he engaged in sexual misconduct and campaign finance violations.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Ruben Gallego, Democrat of Arizona, had denied the accusations and proactively reached out to the Senate Ethics Committee and offered to cooperate with an investigation.

item.title: Sex Abuse Survivors Reach $395 Million Settlement With San Francisco Diocese
item.description: The Archdiocese of San Francisco and lawyers for the survivors said the settlement, which needs final approval, would resolve about 530 claims from people who said they were sexually abused by clergy members.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:credit: Michael Macor/The San Francisco Chronicle, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Archbishop Salvatore Cordileone leads the Archdiocese of San Francisco.

item.title: Colorado Supreme Court Rejects Democratic Redistricting Plans
item.description: The state’s high court found that ballot initiatives designed to change state law to install a newly gerrymandered map violated Colorado law.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Stephen Speranza for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Colorado Supreme Court rejected two ballot initiatives on Monday that would have given voters the chance in November to approve off-year redistricting ahead of 2028 federal elections.

item.title: What to Know About Wildfires in Colorado and Other Western States
item.description: Thousands of acres in Utah and other Western states have burned. Here is what to know about the firefighters who have died, the destruction so far and the spreading smoke.
item.dc:creator: Maia Spoto
item.media:credit: Noah Berger/Associated Press
item.media:description: A helicopter dropped water on the Cottonwood Fire burning near Beaver, Utah, on Saturday.

item.title: Supreme Court Decision on Late-Arriving Mail-In Ballots Grants Relief to 18 States
item.description: The Supreme Court ruled on Monday that state laws allowing ballots to arrive after Election Day are legal. The decision is the latest in a series of setbacks for President Trump’s efforts to regulate elections.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court decision means that states do not have to scramble to change their systems before November or get the word out to voters to return their ballots earlier.

item.title: Inmates Take Guards Hostage in Hourslong Standoff at North Carolina Jail
item.description: It is not yet clear what ignited the takeover, but the county sheriff said it started when at least one inmate got access to keys used to unlock cell doors.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina

item.title: Fired F.T.C. Commissioner Warns of Potential for Presidential Abuse of Power
item.description: Rebecca Slaughter said independent agencies like the Federal Trade Commission were created as watchdogs of powerful corporations and that presidents shouldn’t interfere with that.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rebecca Kelly Slaughter with her lawyer at the Supreme Court in December.

item.title: In Dissents, Some Justices Push Back Against Ruling to Block Trump From Firing Fed Governor Lisa Cook
item.description: A few dissenters cautioned that the majority didn’t allow the legal case over the president’s effort to fire the Fed governor Lisa D. Cook to play out in the lower courts.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lisa Cook in 2025.

item.title: Alex Murdaugh’s Murder Retrial Tentatively Set for April 2027
item.description: The move comes after a court overturned his murder convictions. A judge still needs to decide the location of the trial and what evidence will be allowed.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Tracy Glantz
item.media:description: Alex Murdaugh at a judicial hearing on Monday in Lexington, S.C.

item.title: Why Trump Is Giving Republicans Midterms Anxiety
item.description: Ahead of the midterms, Republicans have sought to focus on the issue of affordability, but the president keeps going off script. Our White House correspondent Tyler Pager explains.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager, Paul Abowd, Jon Miller, Nikolay Nikolov, Gilad Thaler and Whitney Shefte

item.title: Supreme Court Puts Limits on Cellphone Location Data Searches
item.description: The case involved “geofence” searches, which allow law enforcement to find suspects and witnesses by sweeping up location data from cellphones near crime scenes.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow and Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Dakota Santiago for The New York Times
item.media:description: So-called geofence searches have become a popular tool for law enforcement, but critics say they put at risk the personal data of everyday Americans and violate the Constitution.

item.title: ‘No One’s Coming to Save Us but Us’: Gen Z Runs for Office
item.description: Melat Kiros’s challenge Tuesday to Representative Diana DeGette, who was elected the year before Ms. Kiros was born, is the latest Gen Z test in a year defined by generational upheaval.
item.dc:creator: Emma Schartz and Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Chet Strange for The New York Times
item.media:description: Melat Kiros hopes to defeat Representative Diana DeGette, who was elected to her Denver-area seat a year before Ms. Kiros was born.

item.title: Supreme Court Rules Against Roy Moore in Fight Over $8.2 Million Jury Award
item.description: The former Alabama Supreme Court justice and Senate candidate had asked the court to clear a path for him to potentially collect a jury award in a defamation case.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Elijah Nouvelage/Reuters
item.media:description: Roy Moore ran for U.S. Senate in Alabama in a special election in 2017.

item.title: Supreme Court to Weigh if Arizona Can Demand Proof of Citizenship to Vote
item.description: The case could open the door to stricter registration requirements at a time when President Trump has been pushing for them.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Ash Ponders for The New York Times
item.media:description: The new case does not directly address the role of the federal government in demanding proof of citizenship as President Trump has pushed for, but it could lead to states being granted more power to police their own voter rolls.

item.title: Trump Renews Threat to Fire Fed Governor Lisa Cook After Supreme Court Ruling
item.description: President Trump promised to “take appropriate action immediately” against Lisa Cook, the Fed governor he had tried to fire, even as the court affirmed that Fed officials can be fired only for cause.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump renewed his threat to fire Lisa D. Cook.

item.title: ‘Chaos Will Follow’ Ruling Allowing Trump Firings, Dissent Predicts
item.description: In a rare dissent from the bench, Justice Sonia Sotomayor said that the Supreme Court undid “centuries of political practice,” and that the court concluded that the federal government had been acting in “open defiance of the Constitution.”
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak and Aishvarya Kavi
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: Justice Sonia Sotomayor criticized the court for extending its “maximalist view of presidential power.”

item.title: ‘The Daily’: Why everyone cares about this year’s tournament.
item.description: Tariq Panja, a Times reporter, explains the magic of this moment, and two fans of Iran’s soccer team discuss its complexity with Times producer Anna Foley.

item.title: Here’s the latest on the rulings.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow

item.title: See How the Makeup of Independent Agencies Has Changed Under Trump
item.description: Even before the Supreme Court granted him more power to fire officials, President Trump had effectively ended Democratic majorities at several agencies.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella and Karl Russell
item.media:credit: Karl Russell

item.title: Supreme Court Expands Trump’s Power to Fire Officials but Prevents Lisa Cook Removal
item.description: In twin rulings, the Supreme Court affirmed the Fed’s independence and said its leaders could not be fired at will, but said President Trump could fire other independent regulators for any reason.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court ruling has implications for more than two dozen agencies that have traditionally been insulated from presidential control.

item.title: Supreme Court Upholds Grace Period to Count Mail-In Ballots That Arrive After Election Day
item.description: The justices had been asked to examine the legality of the state’s grace period for late-arriving mail-in ballots.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Edmund D. Fountain for The New York Times
item.media:description: A man casts an absentee ballot in Hernando, Miss., last October.

item.title: How A.I. Is Changing the Way Politicians Run for Office
item.description: A.I.-generated images are the public face of this election overhaul. Behind the scenes, campaigns are using the technology to analyze voter data, craft campaign materials and write custom messages.
item.dc:creator: Stuart A. Thompson
item.media:credit: Kriston Jae Bethel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alex Bond speaks about his political views with canvassers from Ground Truth, a door-knocking program organized by Swing Left, a Democratic political group. Canvassers use an A.I.-powered app to record and analyze what voters say.

item.title: Supreme Court Rejects Trump’s Request to Appeal $5 Million Verdict in E. Jean Carroll Case
item.description: President Trump had asked the justices to intervene after a jury found that he had sexually abused and defamed the writer E. Jean Carroll.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle and Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Sarah Blesener for The New York Times
item.media:description: In May 2023, a federal jury in New York found in President Trump liable for sexually abusing and defaming the writer E. Jean Carroll.

item.title: Many Child Safety Features on Social Apps Don’t Work, Report Finds
item.description: Researchers found that teens can discover harmful content, connect with unknown adults and easily bypass time limits.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Valentino-DeVries
item.media:credit: Manuel Ausloos/Reuters
item.media:description: Several tech company leaders are set to testify to Congress next month about harms to children on their platforms.

item.title: The Ohio City Revived by Haitian Immigration Sees an Uncertain Future
item.description: In Springfield, Ohio, some residents see the end of an economic boom after the end of a humanitarian program for immigrants. Others see still darker possibilities.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Amy Powell for The New York Times
item.media:description: Risner, who brought his family to Springfield and bought a home, fears a bleak future in Haiti.

item.title: With Final Decisions Ahead, the Supreme Court Is Sharply Divided
item.description: The justices will decide this week whether President Trump can end the guarantee of birthright citizenship and fire a leader of the independent Federal Reserve.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. Supreme Court in Washington. Its term is set to end this week.

item.title: Israel and Iran Have Divided Democrats and Republicans. Will They Ever Be the Same?
item.description: Raging internal debates over foreign policy threaten both parties’ fortunes in November — and in 2028. Is a major ideological shift underway?
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei and Anton Troianovski
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: In New York’s primaries last week, two incumbent House Democrats lost to primary challengers who had cast them as insufficiently critical of Israel. Pictured are winners Claire Valdez, far left, and Darializa Avila Chevalier, far right, at a pre-primary rally with Mayor Zohran Mamdani.

item.title: Berkeley Will Start Institute Named for Pelosi
item.description: The Trump administration has been scrutinizing the University of California, Berkeley, which insists its new program will be a nonpartisan venture.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Former Speaker Nancy Pelosi and U.C. Berkeley’s chancellor, Richard K. Lyons, at the school’s campus on Saturday.
